35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.

59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70

72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}

76	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}

79	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
80	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
81	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).

87	  %   \end{thm}"

116	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)

120	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is

126	  
127	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
128	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
129	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
130	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
131	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
132	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
133	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
134	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
135	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
136	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
137	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
138	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
139	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
140	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
141	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.

144	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
145	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor

149	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective

155	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
156	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
157	\end{proof}

159	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
160	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
161	
162	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
163	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
164	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
165	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
166	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
167	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
168	
169	\begin{theorem}\leanok
170	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
171	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}

179	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).

202	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
203	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is

237	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
238	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
239	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
240	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
241	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
242	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
243	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
244	  \(k\)-schemes.
245	
246	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
247	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
248	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
249	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
250	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
251	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
252	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
253	  \(k\)-scheme.
254	
255	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
256	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
257	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
258	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
259	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
260	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
261	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
262	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
263	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
264	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
265	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
266	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
267	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
268	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
269	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
270	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
271	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
272	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
273	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
274	  smooth and proper.
275	
276	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
277	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
278	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
279	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
280	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
281	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
282	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
283	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
284	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
285	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
286	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
287	  \(X = C\) complete case.
288	\end{proof}

290	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
291	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
292	
293	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
294	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
295	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
296	representing Picard piece.

298	\begin{lemma}\leanok
299	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
300	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
302	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
303	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
304	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
305	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
306	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
307	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
308	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
309	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
310	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
311	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
312	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
313	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
314	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
315	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
316	  \begin{enumerate}
317	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
318	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
319	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
320	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
321	      and proper map.
322	  \end{enumerate}
323	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
324	  is representable by a smooth map.
325	\end{lemma}
326	
327	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
328	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
329	% structural heart of step 4.)
330	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
331	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
332	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
333	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
334	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
335	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
336	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
337	%
338	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
339	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
340	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
341	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
342	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
343	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
344	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
345	% [\dots]
346	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
347	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
348	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
349	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
350	%
351	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
352	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
353	% [\dots]
354	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
355	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
356	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
357	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
358	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
359	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
360	% represents \(P\)."
361	\begin{proof}
362	
363	\leanok
364	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
365	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
366	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
367	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
368	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
369	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
370	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
371	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
372	  closed embedding.
373	
374	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
375	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
376	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
377	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
378	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
379	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
380	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
381	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
382	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
383	
384	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
385	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
386	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
387	
388	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
389	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
390	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
391	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
392	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
393	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
394	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
395	\end{proof}

397	\section{Group-scheme structure}
398	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
399	
400	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
401	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
402	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
403	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
404	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
405	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
406	
407	\begin{theorem}\leanok
408	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
409	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
410	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
411	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
412	
413	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
414	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
415	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
416	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
417	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
418	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
419	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
422	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
423	  %  topologies  by
424	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
425	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
426	  %   \end{dfn}"
427	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
428	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
429	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
430	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
431	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
432	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
433	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
434	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
435	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
436	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
437	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
438	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
439	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
440	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
441	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
442	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
443	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
444	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
445	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
446	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
447	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
448	  scheme locally of finite type.
449	\end{theorem}
450	
451	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
452	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
453	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
454	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
455	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
456	% records the standard transfer.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
460	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
461	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
462	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
463	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
464	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
465	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
466	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
467	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
468	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
469	  in the value category).
470	
471	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
472	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
473	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
474	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
475	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
476	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
477	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
478	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
479	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
480	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
481	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
482	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
483	\end{proof}

742	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The carrier asserts existence of
743	the Abel map
744	$A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ as a natural
745	transformation of presheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$, sending a relative
746	effective Cartier divisor $D \subseteq C \times_k T$ to its associated
747	invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) = \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$.
748	This is the substantive content of
749	\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.

751	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
752	\begin{itemize}
753	  \item Natural transformation \texttt{${\rightsquigarrow}$} of
754	    functors, \texttt{Nonempty}: \emph{exists}.
755	  \item Dual of an ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$ as an invertible
756	    sheaf for a relative effective Cartier divisor: \emph{partial}.
757	    Mathlib has dual modules and dual sheaves but the specific link
758	    "ideal of a relative effective Cartier divisor is invertible and
759	    its dual is the associated line bundle" is a project-internal
760	    bridge.
761	  \item Functoriality of the assignment under pullback (Kleiman \S3
762	    Lem.~\texttt{lm:ctn}): \emph{needs-bridge}. Pullback of relative
763	    effective divisors along base-change is standard for flat-over-

765	    relative setting is incomplete.
766	\end{itemize}

768	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} None: the Abel map is a
769	construction at the level of line bundles and divisors, not a
770	consequence of Riemann--Roch. (Riemann--Roch tells us that the Abel
771	map for a fixed degree-$g$ pole-set is surjective onto $\Pic^g_{C/k}$;
772	this is a downstream consequence, not a prerequisite for defining the
773	map.)

775	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} The component-wise definition
776	of $A_{C/k}(T)$ is: given $D \in \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)$, set

778	$\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$. Naturality follows from the pullback
779	formula for the dual ideal sheaf. The Lean encoding is a sequence of
780	three constructions: (a) extract the ideal sheaf of a relative
781	effective Cartier divisor, (b) dualise to get the line bundle,
782	(c) pass to the \'etale sheafified class. Each step is independent of
783	Riemann--Roch and depends only on Sorries~1 and~2 having committed to
784	a semantic witness for \texttt{picSharp} and \texttt{divFunctor}.

9	# FGA representability of the Picard scheme (A.2.c)
10	
11	This file is the **A.2.c** assembly file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
12	project's positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It wires the
13	Quot-scheme engine (A.2.b, sibling `Picard/QuotScheme.lean`) and the
14	étale-sheafified relative Picard functor (A.1.c, sibling
15	`Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean`) into the FGA representability statement for
16	`Pic_{C/k}`, following Kleiman, "The Picard scheme", §4 (cf. FGA Explained
17	Ch. 9 §9.4; arXiv:math/0504020).
18	
19	## Status (iter-196 carrier-soundness probe)
20	
21	Each of the six blueprint-pinned declarations carries the *intended* substantive
22	type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
23	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex`). The carrier
24	sorry bodies have been isolated to `Prop`-valued typeclass instance constructors
25	(`⟨sorry⟩`) following the iter-196 carrier-soundness probe (Option A
26	`Functor.IsRepresentable` pattern). The carrier definitions themselves are now
27	`noncomputable` extractions via `Classical.choose` / `Classical.choice` from the
28	typeclass existence fields, so consumers that *quantify* over the typeclasses
29	(rather than use the global instances) are kernel-clean.
30	
31	The 6 pinned declarations are:
32	
33	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **Picard scheme**
34	   `Pic_{C/k}` as an object of `Over (Spec k)`, the witness extracted from
35	   the `Functor.RepresentableBy` produced by `representable`.
36	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap` (def, ~6 LOC) — the
37	   **Abel map** `A_{C/k} : Div_{C/k} ⟶ Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}` as a natural
38	   transformation of presheaves on `(Sch/k)^op`.
39	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient` (theorem, ~15 LOC)
40	   — the Altman–Kleiman **smooth-proper quotient lemma** (Kleiman §4
41	   Lem. `lm:qt`) packaging the descent from a flat-and-proper equivalence
42	   relation `R ⊆ Z ×_k Z` on a quasi-projective `k`-scheme `Z` to a
43	   representing quotient `Q`.
44	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable` (theorem, ~12 LOC) —
45	   the **FGA representability theorem** (Kleiman §4 Thm. `th:main`,
46	   specialised via Cor. `cor:algsch` to `S = Spec k` and `X = C` a smooth
47	   proper geometrically integral curve): the étale-sheafified relative
48	   Picard functor is represented by `PicScheme`.
49	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure` (instance,
50	   ~6 LOC) — the **group-scheme structure** on `PicScheme`: a `GrpObj`
51	   instance obtained by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure
52	   on `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`.

54	## Note on type expressivity
55	
56	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
57	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
58	
59	- `PicScheme C : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a scheme over `k`, not a
60	  tautological `Spec k`.
61	- `abelMap C : divFunctor C ⟶ picSharp C` — a genuine natural
62	  transformation of presheaves, not `𝟙 _` (it sends a relative effective
63	  divisor to its associated invertible sheaf).
64	- `smoothProperQuotient` carries the four Kleiman §4 Lem. `lm:qt`
65	  hypotheses (Z represented, R representable, smooth+proper first
66	  projection, étale surjection) and concludes `P.IsRepresentable`. The
67	  hypotheses are not vacuous: any one of (i)–(iv) can fail for a
68	  non-quotient morphism of presheaves.
69	- `representable C : (picSharp C).RepresentableBy (PicScheme C)` — the

72	- `groupSchemeStructure C : GrpObj (PicScheme C)` — a genuine group-object

180	/-! ## §1. The Picard scheme
181	
182	The Picard scheme `Pic_{C/k}` of a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
183	`C/k` is the `k`-scheme representing the étale-sheafified relative Picard
184	functor `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`. It is supplied by Kleiman §4 Thm. `th:main`
185	(specialised to `S = Spec k`, `X = C` via Cor. `cor:algsch`); the
186	representability theorem itself is the next declaration (`representable`),
187	and `PicScheme` is the witness scheme extracted from it.
188	
189	The scheme is separated, locally of finite type over `k`, and a disjoint
190	union of open quasi-projective `k`-subschemes; the abelian-group-scheme
191	structure is the content of `groupSchemeStructure`.
192	
193	Blueprint reference: `def:pic_scheme` (Kleiman §4 Def. `df:Psch`). -/
194	
195	/-- Typeclass asserting existence of a scheme over `Spec k` that represents
196	`picSharp C`. The single `⟨sorry⟩` instance below is the unique sorry-carrying
197	site for `PicScheme`; consumers that quantify over `[HasPicScheme C]` as a
198	hypothesis remain kernel-clean. -/
199	class HasPicScheme {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
200	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
201	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] : Prop where
202	  has_pic_scheme : ∃ (X : Over (Spec (.of k))),
203	    Nonempty ((PicScheme.picSharp C).RepresentableBy X)
204	
205	/-- The **Picard scheme** `Pic_{C/k}` of a smooth proper geometrically
206	integral curve `C/k`.
207	
208	Encoded as an object of `Over (Spec (.of k))` (a `k`-scheme), carrying the
209	substantive identity "this is the scheme representing `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`"
